titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to family, 11 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his family. Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/96

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Mater
Mat
Edmund
Rex Please Return To Lil Dec.11 My dear folks I am beginning this letter during an easy 10 mins
but do not know when & where I shall finish. I have had 3
separate interruptions since I began! I took a walk today long our river towpath. The river
is the size of the Thames at Oxford but deeper & faster. All the
same the French use it for barges & I overtook an enormous
one towed bt 67 horses. The railway lines alongside so appar-
ently there is room for both in this country. We are all settling down into our new quarters & doing
enquiring works on a large scale quite oblivious of the fact
that we may be called up to the trenches at a moments' notice
However other troops will get the benefit, if we don't, & if everyone
acted on these principles the country would be that more comfortable
& certainly the trenches would in certain cases be better./p> One is occasionally on a strenuous bout of duty, as when
any intraining is on, & I expect to be up Sunday night at
the station, but I find that being without an occasional night's
rest does not hurt one if one is in the open air —a great
score for Martin's open air faith —. At other times I may
find myself confined from 8.15 AM till 12 MN in the office &
dictating fairly hard the whole time except meals, but again one
has begun to grow accustomed to long hours by now, & if only one's
work is good& meets with
occasional appreciation. one does not mind This is not very often the case from the Gen—
eral, but it is not his way, & in any case one does not work for
praise, though it would occasionally grease the wheels. I must try to send you a picture postcard of our chateau
a most imposing looking building, & which is really old this
new modern portion being at least 200 years old. It is rather gloomy
inside except on a sunny day, and the arrangements were not meant
for beauty, i.e. 2 very fine staircases go up the towers where
they are quite lost from a picturesque point of view. I never
realised before that halls and staircases are really a beautiful part
of a house, or can be made so Tomorrow I am to dine with General Remington of
the Indian Cavalry Corps, who as a fervent Ulster man wishes
to see me, and will I fear be disappointed! If all the Home.Service Territorials are now brought into
the army it will certainly mean a big increase out here,
& every one seems to be agreed that it is here that the <lb/decision will have to be made. I shall make no prophecies
as to date as tho' I know nothing officially, I am going to be very
careful indeed in what I write not because I would not trust you all but
to set an example which is badly needed. Would you believe it, I
actually heard a senior officer relating full details of our Naval Intell-
igence system in Germany giving details which would certainly inculpate
those concerned if passed to that Country. This had been told him by a
Naval Intelligence Officer,
who is endangering the lives of our people certainly deserves to
be shot himself. It is the English way & in certain places
the French are equally careless apparently. They are like a lot of old
women gossipers Talking of gossip they say that Winston Churchill has applied to
be made a Brigadier General! I thought he would have the propriety to
remain out here for 2 months as he is without demanding promotion,
but there may be special reasons for his wishing to hurry before
his pals in high places are removed Tomorrow (Sunday) I am doing a railway reconnaissance
so shall probably miss church unless there is an evening service.
Unluckily the padré who has 9 services and 7 sermons in the
day is usually so tired in the evening that he lacks
concentration at the 2 late services. Poor man he like everyone
else has war work to do. No mail has arrived today & I always feel dried up
without it. My best love to all, and again my hearty thanks
to Martin for his hospitality to Lily & his kindness to her
which she appreciated to the full, & I a little less. Wilfrid B Spender
